description_long: This is a three-story brick commercial building with a flat, light-colored roof and ornate cornice detailing along the top edge. The facade features symmetrical rows of arched windows with stone sills and decorative brickwork, giving it a historic, late 19th to early 20th-century urban appearance. The ground floor has a prominent sign reading 'CANADA DRY' and a smaller sign for a 'cafe' or bakery, both mounted above and beside the main entrance. The lower portion of the building is clad in gray stone, contrasting with the red brick above. No awning or outdoor seating is visible, and the windows do not display any baked goods. The overall structure suggests a robust, masonry construction typical of older city blocks.
description: A historic, three-story brick bakery building with arched windows, ornate cornice, and prominent signage on the ground floor. The facade combines red brick and gray stone, with no visible awning or outdoor seating.
description_view_0: Front view showing the main entrance, 'CANADA DRY' sign, and a smaller 'cafe' sign, with three rows of arched windows above.
description_view_1: Side view revealing the building's depth, brickwork, and the protruding edge of the 'CANADA DRY' sign.
description_view_2: Rear or alternate side view with symmetrical arched windows and no signage or entry features.
description_view_3: Back view with plain brickwork and arched windows, lacking signage or entry points.
attribute_storefront_signage: There is a large rectangular sign above the main entrance reading 'CANADA DRY' in red and gold letters on a white background, with a vintage style. A smaller sign below reads 'cafe' in a script font. Both signs are mounted on the stone facade, with the larger sign extending outward from the building.
attribute_architectural_style: The building exhibits features of late 19th to early 20th-century urban commercial architecture, with red brickwork, arched windows, stone sills, and a decorative cornice. The style is reminiscent of Victorian or early Industrial-era city blocks.
